activation_patching.interpretation: Activation patching provides statistically significant causal evidence for feature absorption. Zeroing child features recovers correct letter predictions at rate 0.325 vs. control 0.015 (diff=0.310, Wilcoxon p=0.000218, bootstrap p=0.0, Cohen's d=1.33). N=19 words with absorption, 16 show positive recovery effect.
activation_patching.resolves: Two-interpretation ambiguity (metric calibration vs genuine absorption)

key_findings.finding: Strict vs loose hedging gap for city-language
Strict vs loose hedging gap for first-letter
Strict hedging comparison: city-language vs first-letter

tightened_hedging.implication: Multi-L0 strict hedging is only 7.9% vs loose 94.1% for first-letter. 86.2% of FN resolution is compensatory (non-parent features).

cmi_l0_22.key_finding: rho=0.044, p=0.83. CMI does not predict absorption. Demoted to appendix.

threshold_sensitivity.one_line: The control failure (false negatives after SAE encoding) is structural, not threshold-dependent. Thresholds only affect classification of existing failures, not the failures themselves.
for_paper_appendix: Table: 5x4 heatmap of absorption rates (cosine threshold x magnitude gap)
Finding: CV=0.079, all 20 cells in [11.8%, 15.1%] range
Finding: False negatives constant (n=87/576) across all threshold settings
Finding: Perfect monotonicity in both dimensions
Conclusion: Absorption measurement is threshold-robust; control failure is structural

findings: city-continent: F1=0.8428 at L24 (below strict, above 0.80)
city-country: F1=0.7895 at L24 (below relaxed gate)
city-language: F1=0.8234 at L24 (below strict, above 0.80)
first-letter: F1=0.9711 at L24 (strict gate PASS)
Layer 24 consistently best for all RAVEL hierarchies
probe_training.paper_implication: First-letter (F1=0.97) most reliable; city-continent (0.84) and city-language (0.82) acceptable with caveats; city-country (0.79) marginal.

findings: Layer 24 dramatically higher absorption (25-35%) vs L6/L12/L18 (2-9%)
65k-width SAEs slightly higher absorption at L12 and L24
L24 rates (25-35%) align with Chanin et al. published 15-35%
Absorption concentrates at final prediction layers -- novel finding
Absorption rates differ significantly across hierarchies
Layer and hierarchy type both matter for absorption
absorption_crossdomain.paper_implication: Absorption is both hierarchy-dependent AND layer-dependent.

gas_full.failure_analysis: GAS captures decoder geometry but NOT encoder competitive exclusion dynamics.

tax_qualitative.finding: T(G) ranking does NOT predict observed absorption ranking. R_pc-absorption correlations near zero.

architecture_comparison.h6_detail: No significant architecture effect. Hierarchy type matters more than architecture.
caveats: All at layer 12 (only layer with all architectures available)
RAVEL probes below strict gate at layer 12

hypothesis_verdicts.name: Cross-Domain Variation
hypothesis_verdicts.key_evidence: Absorption rates differ significantly across hierarchies (ANOVA p=0.005). At L24: first-letter (34.5%) vs city-country (18.5%, p=0.004) vs city-language (13.6%, p=0.0001). 4/6 pairwise comparisons significant.

hypothesis_verdicts.name: Semantic > First-Letter
hypothesis_verdicts.key_evidence: At layer 24, first-letter shows HIGHEST absorption (34.5%), not semantic hierarchies. City-country (18.5%) and city-language (13.6%) are LOWER. This reverses the pilot finding at L12. Layer dependence dominates.
hypothesis_verdicts.note: Stronger finding than expected: absorption is layer-dependent AND hierarchy-dependent with different patterns at different layers.

hypothesis_verdicts.name: Absorption-Hedging Decomposition
hypothesis_verdicts.key_evidence: Multi-L0 first-letter: strict 7.9%, compensatory 86.2%, persistent 5.9%. The 98.6% loose hedging figure is near-tautological.

hypothesis_verdicts.name: GAS Detector
hypothesis_verdicts.key_evidence: rho=0.116, AUROC=0.571, CI includes zero. 25x scale-up from pilot confirms signal absent.

hypothesis_verdicts.name: Absorption Tax Predictions
hypothesis_verdicts.key_evidence: T(G) ranking rho=-0.20, concordance ~50% (chance). R_pc correlations near zero at most configs.

hypothesis_verdicts.name: Architecture Generalization
hypothesis_verdicts.key_evidence: No significant architecture effect (p=0.87). Hierarchy effect significant (p=0.0050). Hierarchy type matters more than architecture choice.

hypothesis_verdicts.name: Causal Absorption
hypothesis_verdicts.key_evidence: n=25 words, recovery diff=0.310, Wilcoxon p=0.000218, Cohen's d=1.33 (large). 16/19 words show positive recovery. CI excludes zero: [0.213, 0.421].

writing_gate.rationale: Primary contribution (cross-domain absorption differences) has statistical evidence (4/6 comparisons significant, ANOVA p=0.005). Causal evidence (activation patching) is statistically significant (Wilcoxon p=0.000218, d=1.33). Probe quality is acceptable with documented caveats (best RAVEL F1=0.84, first-letter F1=0.97).

paper_framing_update.original: Semantic hierarchies show MORE absorption than first-letter (pilot at L12)
paper_framing_update.revised: Absorption is LAYER-DEPENDENT and HIERARCHY-DEPENDENT. At L24, first-letter shows HIGHEST absorption (34.5%), while cross-domain hierarchies show lower rates (13-36%). This represents significant cross-domain variation (ANOVA p=0.005).
paper_framing_update.implication: Paper must be reframed: primary contribution is demonstrating significant cross-domain variation with the surprising layer-hierarchy interaction.

required_caveats: RAVEL probes below strict quality gate (F1 0.79-0.84); absolute rates have uncertainty
GAS, CMI, Absorption Tax are negative results -- appendix only
Architecture comparison at layer 12 only; cross-domain probes below gate at that layer
Activation patching probe F1=0.883 (below strict 0.90 gate)

negative_results.result: H2' refuted: semantic NOT > first-letter at L24
RAVEL probes below strict quality gate

positive_results.result: Causal absorption confirmed via activation patching
positive_results.evidence: 32.5% recovery vs 1.5% control, Wilcoxon p=0.000218, d=1.33

positive_results.result: Probe quality strongly predicts false negative rate

key_claims: First systematic cross-domain absorption characterization on Gemma 2 2B
Absorption is hierarchy-dependent (ANOVA p=0.005) and layer-dependent (2-35%)
First causal evidence via activation patching: 32.5% vs 1.5% (p=0.000218, d=1.33)

section3_probes.quality_table: 20 probes across 4 hierarchies x multiple layers

section4_crossdomain.layer_dependence: Novel: absorption concentrates at final prediction layers (L24 >> L6/L12/L18)
section4_crossdomain.statistics: 4/6 pairwise comparisons significant at p<0.05; ANOVA p=0.005
section4_crossdomain.key_insight: First-letter shows highest absorption at L24 (34.5%), reversing L12 pilot finding

section5_mechanism.activation_patching: n=25, recovery 32.5% vs 1.5%, Wilcoxon p=0.000218, d=1.33

section6_architecture.note: Underpowered -- small samples + probe quality at L12

appendix.threshold: Structural, CV=0.077, FN constant across grid
